items.text: Chapter Two
Identification of Tuta absoluta and the Nature of Its Damage
Introduction

Tuta absoluta is considered one of the most serious insect pests attacking tomato crops, both in open fields and protected cultivation. It has gained this status because of its ability to infest multiple plant parts, its rapid reproduction, the concealed feeding habit of its larvae within plant tissues, the ease with which adult moths move between fields, and its capacity to develop resistance to several insecticides.

The severity of Tuta absoluta is not measured solely by the number of insects present in the field, but rather by its ability to cause direct damage within leaves, stems, growing points, and fruits. During the early stages of infestation, the plant may appear externally to be in acceptable condition, while larvae may already have begun forming mines within its tissues.
Therefore, the nature of the insect, the damaging stage, and the mechanism of infestation must be understood before establishing any monitoring or control program.
Identification of Tuta absoluta

Tuta absoluta is a small moth belonging to the order Lepidoptera, which includes butterflies and moths. It belongs to the small-sized family Gelechiidae, which includes many species whose larvae are characterized by boring or feeding within plant tissues.
The commonly used scientific name is:
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick)
Some modern taxonomic references also use the name:
Phthorimaea absoluta (Meyrick)

The difference in genus name among some sources does not mean that two different pests are being discussed; rather, it results from taxonomic revisions concerning the relationships among the genera within this group. The name Tuta absoluta remains the most widely used name in agricultural guides, control programs, pheromone traps, and agricultural trade.
The insect undergoes complete metamorphosis, meaning that it passes through four main stages:

Each stage performs a different function within the life cycle. The egg represents the beginning of the generation, the larva is the feeding, growth, and damaging stage, the pupa is the transformation stage into the adult insect, while the moths are responsible for mating, dispersal, and egg laying.
Tomato is the principal economic host of the pest, although it can infest other plants, particularly some species belonging to the family Solanaceae.
Is Tuta absoluta a Moth, a Miner, or a Worm?
These terms describe different aspects of the insect:

items: Larva or caterpillar: the stage that emerges from the egg and feeds on the plant.
Miner: a description of the larva’s feeding behavior within plant tissues.
Tomato leaf miner: a common name for the pest, but it does not reflect all forms of damage it causes.
Tomato borer or tomato fruit borer: refers to its ability to penetrate fruits and stems.
Weevil: a colloquial name used by some growers, but it is taxonomically inaccurate because the insect is not a true weevil belonging to the order Coleoptera.

items.text: Therefore, Tuta absoluta is not a “worm” throughout its life. It is a moth whose larval stage is worm-like, and this is the stage that mines within the plant and causes damage.
Nature of Infestation

Infestation usually begins when the female lays eggs on plant parts, after which the egg hatches and a small larva emerges. Following hatching, the larva searches for a suitable penetration point and rapidly enters the leaf, stem, bud, or fruit.
Once the larva settles within plant tissue, it begins feeding and forms a mine or mined blotch. The appearance of the damage varies according to the infested plant part and larval age.
Tuta absoluta is a pest that:

items: Can cause both direct and indirect damage.
Can infest the plant from the nursery stage until close to harvest.

items.text: For this reason, describing it solely as a “leaf miner” is incomplete, because the most severe economic losses are often associated with infestation of growing points, stems, and fruits.
Nature of the Damage Caused by the Pest
The damage caused by Tuta absoluta can be divided into five interrelated types:

items: 4. Indirect damage resulting from secondary infection.

items.text: Direct Feeding Damage

Direct damage occurs when larvae feed on plant tissues. In leaves, the larva consumes the mesophyll tissue located between the two epidermal layers, while the two outer epidermal layers may remain temporarily intact.
This results in the formation of pale or translucent areas known as:
Mines or mined blotches — Mines
As feeding continues and the mine expands, the infested area gradually turns brown, then dries out and dies.
In stems, petioles, and growing points, larvae feed within the tissues and form tunnels that may affect the movement of water and nutrients and the continued growth of young plant parts.
In fruits, the larva enters beneath the epidermis or into the fleshy tissues, forming tunnels that are usually associated with feeding residues and frass.
Physiological Damage
The effect of leaf infestation is not limited to the presence of dry blotches; it also results in a reduction in the effective leaf area capable of carrying out photosynthesis.
When infestation is severe, it may lead to:

items: Reduced sugar production within the plant.
Declining ability of the plant to fill fruits.
Fruit exposure to sunlight due to canopy collapse.
Increased risk of sunscald and elevated fruit temperature.
Reduced plant capacity to withstand heat and water stress.

items.text: The severity of this effect depends on the proportion of infested leaves, the location of infestation, plant age, timing of damage, and the extent to which the canopy continues to produce new leaves.

Not every leaf mine represents the same level of loss. Infestation of an old lower leaf is not equivalent to infestation of a group of young leaves or the growing point during fruit set and fruit-filling stages.
Structural Damage to Stems and Growing Points
Larvae may bore into:
Infestation of the growing point in young plants can produce consequences more serious than the loss of part of a leaf, because it may cause:

items: Disruption of the balance between vegetative and reproductive growth.
Poor formation of early fruit clusters.
Loss of part of the early yield.

items.text: Tunnels within the stem may also weaken the tissue and cause wilting of the plant part beyond the infested zone, particularly when more than one larva is present or when infestation overlaps with plant pathogens.
Direct Damage to Fruits
Fruit infestation is the most economically serious form of damage because, in this case, the pest directly reaches the harvested part that is sold or delivered to the processing plant.
Larvae may enter fruits:

items: At points of contact between fruits and leaves.
Through the sides of the fruit.
Through cracks or weak areas in the epidermis.
Sometimes through the lower part of the fruit or from areas that are not externally obvious.

items.text: Infested fruits may show signs such as:

items: Discoloration of tissues surrounding the tunnel.
Presence of the larva or its shed skin inside the fruit.
Development of rots around the infestation site.

items.text: The entry hole may be extremely small compared with the extent of internal damage. Therefore, a rapid external inspection is not sufficient to determine fruit soundness, particularly when evaluating produce intended for processing or export.
Indirect Damage and Secondary Infection
The tunnels, holes, and wounds produced by larvae provide entry points for pathogens, especially fungi and bacteria that cause rots.
Thus, the problem can develop from limited insect infestation into complex deterioration involving:

items: 1. A wound caused by larval entry.
3. Accumulation of moisture and frass inside the tunnel.

items.text: Rots may continue to develop during harvesting, transport, and temporary storage. Consequently, losses may continue after the crop has left the field.
This problem becomes more severe under warm and humid conditions, when fruits are piled, transport is delayed, and additional mechanical injuries are present.
Economic and Marketability Damage

Infestation does not have to cause fruit drop or complete fruit loss before an economic loss is recorded. The fruit may remain on the plant and its weight may be included in total yield, yet it may be unsuitable for marketing.
Forms of economic loss include:

items: Rejection of infested fruits in the fresh market.
Rejection of entire loads when infestation levels are high.
Increased losses at the processing plant.
Higher microbial load in the raw material.
Reduced quantity of sound fruit suitable for processing.
Increased number of sprays and higher control costs.
Reduced profits for both growers and processors.

items.text: Therefore, the field should be evaluated using marketable yield, not total fruit weight alone.
The Damaging Stage
The Larva Is the Stage Responsible for Damage
The larval stage is the damaging stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta.

The larva passes through several instars or molts, and the amount of tissue it consumes increases as it develops. Newly hatched larvae begin by forming small mines, which expand as they grow. The larva may move from one mine to another or from a leaf to another plant part.
All larval instars are capable of causing damage, but the nature of the damage differs among them:
Newly Hatched Larvae
They are characterized by being:

items: Rapid in searching for a penetration site.
Able to initiate tiny mines that are difficult to detect.
Potentially more susceptible to control during the short period between hatching and tissue penetration.
Important targets for biological insecticides and products acting through contact or ingestion.

items.text: Intermediate and Advanced Larval Instars
They are characterized by:

items: Consuming a larger quantity of tissue.
Being able to attack stems and fruits.
Causing most of the visible damage.
Being better protected within plant tissues.
Potentially leaving one mine and moving to a new site.

items.text: Therefore, control should not be delayed until large larvae and wide mines become visible, because this means that a substantial proportion of the damage has already occurred.
Why Is the Egg Not Considered a Damaging Stage?
The egg does not feed on the plant and therefore causes no direct damage. However, it is an important early indicator of the presence of a new generation before damage begins.

Egg monitoring is useful because controlling or parasitizing eggs before hatching prevents larval emergence altogether. This explains the importance of egg parasitoids such as Trichogramma species and of inspecting young leaves before mines appear.
The egg is not a damaging stage, but it is a critical stage for prevention.
Why Is the Pupa Not Considered a Damaging Stage?
The pupa is a non-feeding stage during which the larva transforms into an adult moth. It therefore causes no direct loss of plant tissue.

However, the presence of pupae in the soil, leaves, or crop residues means that a new generation of moths will emerge after a period of time. For this reason, residue management, tillage, and removal of infested plants are important components in reducing pest sources.
Why Is the Adult Moth Not Considered the Direct Damaging Stage?
Adult moths, if they feed at all, feed on fluids and do not bore into tomato tissues or produce the mines found in leaves and fruits.
Therefore, the moth does not cause the visible damage directly, but it has major epidemiological importance because it is responsible for:

items: Spread of the pest over new distances.

items.text: Accordingly, the moth can be described as the dispersal and reproductive stage, whereas the larva is the damaging stage.
This distinction is essential when interpreting pheromone-trap catches. The trap measures adult male activity and does not directly count the larvae present inside the plant.
Step-by-Step Mechanism of Damage
The infestation sequence can be simplified as follows:
Stage One: Arrival of Moths in the Field
Moths may enter from:

items: Contaminated boxes or transport equipment in some cases.

items.text: Stage Two: Egg Laying
Females lay eggs on suitable plant parts, especially young tissues and leaves. Eggs may also be found near stems, the calyx, and fruits.
Stage Three: Hatching
A very small larva emerges from the egg and begins searching for a penetration site.
Stage Four: Tissue Penetration
The larva mines into the leaf, stem, or fruit and, after entering, becomes less exposed to external influences.
Stage Five: Mine Expansion
The larva continues feeding and growing, the damaged area expands, and frass accumulates within the mine or around openings.
Stage Six: Movement
The larva may leave the mine and move to another leaf, stem, or fruit. Therefore, the presence of an empty mine does not mean that infestation has ended.
Stage Seven: Pupation and Emergence of a New Generation
After completing its growth, the larva transforms into a pupa, from which a new moth emerges, lays eggs, and repeats the infestation cycle.
Overlapping generations result in eggs, larvae, pupae, and moths being present in the field at the same time. This complicates control and prevents reliance on a single treatment.
Why Is It Difficult for an Insecticide to Reach the Larva?

After the larva penetrates the tissue, it becomes partially surrounded by the layers of the leaf, stem, or fruit. Therefore, the spray solution may cover the external plant surface while failing to reach the larva in an effective quantity.
Control becomes more difficult when:

items: The larva is at an advanced instar.
It is located deep within the fruit.
The spray does not reach both the upper and lower leaf surfaces.
The product used is unsuitable for the target stage.
Spraying is delayed until mines have expanded.
Resistance to the active ingredient is present.

items.text: For this reason, timing of intervention is extremely important. It is preferable to target the period immediately before or after hatching, before most larvae become established within plant tissues.
However, this timing cannot be determined from traps alone. Trap data must be integrated with inspection of eggs, recent mines, larvae, and infested plants.
General Concept of Pest Severity
A Successful Invasive Pest
Tuta absoluta possesses several characteristics that have enabled it to develop from a local pest in South America into a widely distributed international pest, including:

items: Ability to move with plant products.
Capacity for flight and local dispersal.
Adaptation to a wide range of environmental conditions.
Rapid colonization of new production areas.

items.text: The pest exerts particularly high pressure in areas of continuous or overlapping tomato cultivation, where there is no sufficiently long host-free period.
Crop Infestation at All Growth Stages
Infestation may begin in the nursery and continue until the end of harvest. Consequently, the form of loss varies according to crop stage:

| Crop Stage | Potential Effect of Infestation | | ----------- | ------------------------------- | | Nursery | Weak transplants and transfer of the pest to the field | | After transplanting | Slow establishment and infestation of growing points | | Vegetative growth | Reduction in leaf area | | Flowering and fruit set | Disruption of growth and infestation of young tissues | | Fruit filling | Reduced photosynthesis and fruit infestation | | Coloring and harvest | Fruit rejection, increased losses, and preharvest-interval problems |
This extended period means that pest management must be a continuous program, not a curative spray applied only after damage appears.
Overlapping Generations
Under favorable conditions, the field may contain at the same time:
A particular treatment may therefore affect one stage while the remaining stages survive and rebuild the population after a short period.
Differences in plant age and multiple planting dates within the production area also allow the pest to continue moving from one field to another.
Capacity to Cause Severe Losses

In severe outbreaks that are not detected early and are not managed effectively, a very large proportion of the crop may be damaged, and the loss of marketable yield may approach complete crop loss in extreme cases.
However, the estimate of “100% losses” should not be applied generally to every infestation. Actual losses vary according to several factors, including:
Early presence of the pest does not inevitably mean crop loss, but it requires a rapid response based on monitoring.
Capacity to Develop Resistance
In many regions, populations of Tuta absoluta are exposed to strong selection pressure due to repeated insecticide use.

With multiple generations and large numbers of individuals, the probability increases that some individuals capable of tolerating a particular active ingredient will survive. These individuals then reproduce, reducing the susceptibility of the pest population to that insecticide.
Resistance increases when:

items: The same active ingredient is repeatedly used.
The same IRAC group is repeatedly used.
Different trade names belonging to the same group are repeatedly used.
The dose is reduced without a technical basis.
Spraying is conducted at an unsuitable time.
Migration continues from fields using different spray programs.
A large number of treatments is applied during the season.

items.text: Insecticide resistance is one of the principal reasons why Tuta absoluta remains a difficult and persistently serious pest.
Movement from Old Fields to Newly Planted Fields
A field that has completed harvesting may become a major source of moths if infested plants are left without shredding or plowing.
A dangerous scenario occurs when:

items: 1. Infestation increases in an old field.
4. Infested plants remain in the field.
5. New moths emerge in large numbers.
7. Infestation begins in newly planted crops.

items.text: Therefore, crop-residue management is not a cosmetic practice, but a direct method of breaking the pest cycle and protecting neighboring fields.
Low Acceptance Threshold for Infested Fruits
The plant may tolerate a limited amount of leaf infestation, but an infested fruit is usually not commercially acceptable.
A single hole may result in:

items: An increased percentage of defects in the load.

items.text: Therefore, the economic threshold for fruit infestation is much lower than the threshold associated with the presence of a limited number of leaf mines.
In processing tomatoes, it is necessary to distinguish between:

items: Yield accepted by the processing plant.
Percentage of infested, rotten, or rejected fruits.

items.text: Difference Between Active and Old Infestation
Seeing a mine on a leaf does not necessarily mean that a live larva is still present inside it at the time of inspection. The larva may have:

items: Left and moved to another leaf.
Completed its development and left to pupate.

items.text: Therefore, several mines should be opened and examined to determine whether infestation is active.
Indicators of Active Infestation

items: Presence of newly laid eggs near growing tissues.

items.text: Indicators of Old Infestation

items: No further expansion of the mine.

items.text: Confusing old and active infestation leads to inaccurate spray decisions. Insecticides may be applied because of old damage that contains no live larvae, or the field may be neglected because adult moth catches are low despite the presence of active larvae inside plants.
Indicators That Should Be Monitored to Assess Risk
Tuta absoluta should not be evaluated using a single indicator. The best assessment combines:

items: Number of moths caught in traps.
Trend in trap catches from one week to the next.
Percentage of plants with recent mines.
Distribution of infestation within the field.
Severity of infestation at field edges compared with the interior.
Presence of neighboring old or neglected fields.
Presence of natural enemies or parasitized eggs.
Crop age and time remaining until harvest.

items.text: Risk increases when rising trap catches coincide with the presence of new eggs, recent mines, live larvae, and infested fruits.
Common Misconceptions
Misconception One: Tuta absoluta Attacks Leaves Only
Incorrect. It can infest leaves, petioles, stems, growing points, and fruits.
Misconception Two: The Moth Is the Stage That Bores into Fruits
Incorrect. The larva bores into fruits, while the moth lays eggs and spreads the infestation.
Misconception Three: Failure to See Moths Means the Field Is Pest-Free
Incorrect. Moths are small and their activity may increase at specific times, while larvae remain concealed within the plant.
Misconception Four: High Trap Catch Equals the Same Percentage of Damage
Inaccurate. The trap measures male moth activity, whereas damage depends on eggs, larvae, their location, and crop age.
Misconception Five: Every Mine Requires Immediate Spraying
Inaccurate. It is necessary to determine whether the mine is recent and contains a live larva, and to assess the distribution of infestation and the other field indicators.
Misconception Six: Changing the Trade Name Prevents Resistance
Incorrect. Several trade names may contain the same active ingredient or belong to the same IRAC group and mode of action.
Misconception Seven: Increasing the Number of Sprays Means Better Control
Incorrect. Random spraying may increase resistance, residues, and cost, and may kill natural enemies without achieving sustainable control.
Chapter Summary

Tuta absoluta is an invasive moth whose larval stage is responsible for direct damage. Larvae begin feeding within leaves and can also attack growing points, stems, and fruits, resulting in overlapping physiological, marketability, and economic damage.

The egg and pupa do not cause feeding damage, while the adult moth represents the reproductive and dispersal stage. Distinguishing among these stages is essential for selecting the appropriate control method and timing.
The overall severity of the pest results from the combination of several factors:

items: Concealment of larvae within plant tissues.
Continued infestation from the nursery through harvest.
Susceptibility of infested fruits to decay and rejection.
High capacity to develop insecticide resistance.
Difficulty of relying on a single control method.

items.text: Accordingly, detecting the pest only after mines have spread and fruits have become infested is considered relatively late detection. True success begins by preventing its entry with transplants, monitoring moths and eggs, detecting young larvae before they become established within plant tissues, and then applying an integrated program that combines cultural, biological, behavioral, and chemical measures.
References
